steps.title: Understand the conclusion
steps.content: The researchers concluded that the effect of floral damage varied by both floral morph (pin vs. thrum) and pollinator genus. Supporting data must show different responses to damage depending on both variables at once.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'For pin flowers, damage led to longer times per flower in all pollinator genera, whereas for thrum flowers, damage led to longer times per flower only in Habropoda and Osmia.': Pins showed a uniform increase across all genera, but thrums showed a genus-dependent split — exactly demonstrating both morph and genus variation. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Compared with pollinators belonging to the genus Osmia, pollinators belonging to the genus Xylocopa spent less time on damaged pin flowers but more time on damaged thrum flowers.': Compares only two genera on damaged flowers without addressing the contrast between intact and damaged visits, so it does not support the damage-effect conclusion. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Damage led to shorter times per thrum flower in three pollinator genera (Osmia, Pierid, and Xylocopa), whereas it led to longer times per thrum flower in one pollinator genus (Habropoda).': Misreads the table (only two genera decreased on thrum, not three) and ignores pin flowers entirely, so it cannot support a conclusion about both morphs. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Pollinators belonging to the genus Habropoda spent 2.7 seconds on intact pin flowers and 4.1 seconds on intact thrum flowers.': Describes time on intact flowers for one genus only; it says nothing about the effect of damage across genera or morphs. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
